Name: inter_layer_dependency_aware_mixed_precision
Title: Inter-Layer Dependency Aware Mixed Precision Quantization for Training-Free Model Optimization
Short Hypothesis: Quantization strategies that incorporate inter-layer dependencies can enhance mixed-precision quantization performance, leading to increased model efficiency and reduced accuracy loss without requiring retraining.
Related Work: Recent works like ILD-MPQ and InfoQ have explored mixed precision quantization, but they either rely on learning-based approaches or fail to utilize the full potential of inter-layer dependencies. Our proposal uniquely combines these aspects by establishing a training-free framework that quantifies layer interactions and directly optimizes bit-width allocations.
Abstract: In this work, we present a novel framework for mixed-precision quantization that explicitly considers inter-layer dependencies, enhancing the effectiveness of quantization strategies without necessitating retraining. Existing methods often treat layers independently, risking suboptimal configurations that compromise model accuracy. Our approach leverages a global sensitivity analysis based on mutual information flow to quantify the impact of layer quantization on overall model performance. By formulating the bit-width allocation problem as an optimization task that respects these dependencies, we can derive efficient quantization strategies that maintain high accuracy at reduced computational costs. We will evaluate our method across various architectures, including ResNet and MobileNet, using standard benchmarks to demonstrate its efficacy and efficiency. Our findings will contribute significantly to the mixed-precision quantization landscape, offering a robust solution that aligns with the growing demand for resource-efficient deep learning.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline mixed-precision quantization approach to establish a control for comparison.
2. Develop the inter-layer dependency framework that captures the interactions between layers, utilizing mutual information metrics.
3. Execute a series of experiments comparing traditional independent layer quantization versus our proposed dependency-aware method using MobileNet and ResNet architectures.
4. Evaluate performance using metrics such as accuracy drop, computational efficiency (FLOPs), and memory usage across various bit-width configurations.
5. Perform ablation studies to assess the contribution of inter-layer dependency awareness to overall model performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The primary risk is the potential complexity of accurately modeling inter-layer dependencies, which may introduce unforeseen computational overhead. Additionally, while we aim for a training-free approach, some degree of empirical fine-tuning may still be necessary to optimize performance across different architectures.
